heading: Introduction
text:

Horowitz graduated - got hired Atlanta Hawks - nflscrapR package taken fellow CMU student Ron Yorko (go receive Ph.D. Statistics Data Science program). trio’s work nflscrapR ultimately led peer-reviewed paper titled “nflWAR: Reproducible Method Offensive Player Evaluation Football.” Ultimately, nflscrapR project came end specific .json feed used gather NFL data changed. point, Ben Baldwin Sebastian Carl already built upon nflscrapR project’s foundations create nflfastR. Ron officially marked end nflscrapR era beginning nflfastR era tweet September 14, 2020:1As reply first tweet nflfastR project, Ben explained created original function scrape NFL data creation NFL analytics website. Thankfully, Seb keep creation released nflfastR public. “open source” nature R R packages, laundry list companion packages quickly developed alongside nflfastR. original nflfastR package now part larger nflverse packages drive NFL analytics community Twitter beyond.creation nflverse allowed anybody interested NFL analytics easily access data, manipulate liking, release visualizations /metrics wider public. fact, now regular occurrence somebody advance R programming ability nflverse go win Big Data Bowl. 2022 version Big Data Bowl, “30 participants hired work data analytics roles sports, including 22 hired football” (“Big Data Bowl: Annual Analytics Contest Explores Statistical Innovations Football,” n.d.). recently, Chargers hired 2020 participate Alex Stern Chiefs hired Marc Richards, member winning 2021 team, Football Research Analyst.Kevin Clark, 2018 article Ringer, explained despite obvious sabermetrics movement baseball, analytics movement NFL “happening front time.” use analytics NFL , however, predate Clark’s article. 2014, Eagles head coach Doug Pederson explained decisions made organization - game planning draft strategy - informed hard data analytics. Leading early adoption analytics, reporting directly team Vice President Howie Roseman, Joe Douglas Alec Halaby, “31-year-old Harvard grad job description” emphasis “integrating traditional analytical methods football decision-making.” result? “blending old-school scouting newer approaches” often seen leagues, NBA MLB (Rosenthal 2018). Pederson believed trusted team’s approach analytics much direct line communication created two games, analytics department providing head coach math-based recommendations scenario Pederson requested (Awbrey 2020).2In just five years time since publishing article, become hard ignore analytic movement within NFL. Yet, still much growth happen marriage NFL advanced metrics. example, denying sabermetrics movement drastically “altered baseball’s DNA” Heifetz (2019)]. Moreover, explained Seth Partnow’s outstanding Midrange Theory: Basketball’s Evolution Age Analytics, analytics movement NBA essentially killed midrange shot (briefly: beneficial try work ball directly basket (high-percentage shot) take 3-pointer, possible additional point statistically worth despite lower success probability opposed 2-point midrange shot) well traditional, “old-school” center position.Compared NBA MLB, NFL playing catch analytics driving changes equivalent death midrange shot plethora additional tactics changes baseball sabermetrics. Joe Banner, served President Eagles 2001-2012 Chief Executive Officer Browns 2012-2013, explained hesitation incorporate analytics NFL game planning result game “much driven conventional wisdom extreme degree” (Fortier 2020). Perhaps nobody encapsulates better Pittsburgh Steelers Head Coach Mike Tomlin. asked position analytics 2015 season, Tomlin explained:think ’s play game. can take analytics baseball things like football always going football. got lot respect analytics numbers, ’m going make judgements based numbers. game game. ’s emotional one played emotional driven men. ’s element game can’t measure. Often times decisions weigh heavily equation (Kozora 2015).Given Tomlin’s quote 2015, perhaps Steelers pivoted since now analytically inclined. seem case. poll NFL analytics staffers conducted ESPN, Steelers voted one least analytically advanced teams league.large gap least analytically inclined teams (Washington, Tennessee, Cincinnati, New York Giants, Pittsburgh) voted analytically inclined (Baltimore, Cleveland, Philadelphia, Houston). ESPN poll, Browns voted analytics department producing highest level work. One polled spoke fact much outstanding work result General Manager Andrew Berry “true believer,” explaining one “rare guys ’ll come across life think , ‘Man, guy thinks different level. Just pure genius.’ ’s one .”article Washington Post, Sam Fortier argues many teams became inspired intimately introduce analytics game planning -field decisions 2017 season. run becoming Super Bowl Champions, Philadelphia Eagles aggressive 4th , going 26 times season converting 17 conversion percentage 65.4%. quick examination visualization data highlights absolutely staggering increase 4th aggressiveness among NFL head coaches 2017-2021:
Figure 0.1: 4th Attempts: 2000 - 2021
96.3% increase number 4th attempts just 2017 2021. fact, numbers may actually higher quite conservative building plot considering 4th attempts took place offensive team 5--95% winning probability prior two-minute warning either half. Even conservative limitations, increase staggering. numbers, however, support aggression. week one 2020 2021 season, going 4th “cost teams cumulative 170 percentage points win probability” (Bushnell 2021).Ben Baldwin, using nfl4th package part nflverse, tracked shift NFL coaching mentality regarding 4th decisions comparing 2014’s “go percentage” 2020. compared 2014 season, NFL coaches now much agreement analytics “go ” 4th relation expected gain win probability.
Figure 0.2: Credit: Ben Baldwin
surprising , considering Mike Tomlin’s quote NFL analytics staffers voting Steelers one least analytically driven teams league, Pittsburgh lost win probability either kicking punting “go ” situations 2020 NFL season. end, Ravens Browns - two teams voted analytically inclined - two best organizations knowing “go ” 4th based win probability added. seems defined relationship teams buying analytics :
Figure 0.3: Credit: Ben Baldwin
NFL’s turn towards aggressive 4th-decisions just one many analytics-driven changes occurring league. Another significant example Defense-Adjusted Value Average (DVOA), formula created Aaron Schatz, now editor chief Football Outsiders, sought challenge notion teams , first, establish running game order open passing game. changes apparent televisions screens Sunday afternoons Fall, others occurring behind scenes (analytics departments working scouting draft preparation, example). Indeed, use analytics NFL tightly ingrained see prominent leagues. must remember certainly continued hold outs among NFL coaches (like Mike Tomlin).Despite coaching hold outs fully embracing analytics, “thirst knowledge football excessive sport desire get wins per dollar just high.” pipeline data continues grow, internally league data becomes publicly accessible, “smart teams continue leave rock unturned push limits far data can take .” Joe Banner explained NFL long league coaches saying “well, way ’ve always done ,” league ripe major shift (Bechtold 2021).Banner’s belief teams searching every competitive advantage “leave rock unturned” driving force behind book. intents purposes, age analytics NFL still infancy. Turning back, , 2017 season, Eagles’ management praised credited team’s analytics department part reason able win Super Bowl LII. Danney Heifetz argues, “changed language football.” NFL, explains, “copycat league” , witnessed increase 4th aggressiveness since 2017, teams immediately began carbon copy Philadelphia’s approach folding traditional football strategy new age analytics approach. modernity relationship long-held football dogmas analytics, nobody can quite sure impacts create gamesmanship football.However, Heifetz opines, NBA MLB can serve roadmap analytics take NFL. Importantly, NFL’s relationship analytics still “first frontier likely sweeping change next two decades.” , sure next major impact analytics make, may occur. , ever-growing amount publicly accessible data, matter time discovered. example, interview Heifetz, Brian Burke - one forefather’s NFL analytics now writer ESPN - expressed belief next major impact “quantifying often quarterbacks make correct decision field.”seems every new NFL season results amateur analyst bringing groundbreaking model /approach table. Unlike, example, MLB little left discover terms sabermetrics new approaches understanding game associated strategy, NFL - lack better phrase - open playing field. data becoming available public, now easier ever investigate ideas suspicions create models confirm intuition.example, originally greater Pittsburgh area big Steelers fan (certainly explains Steelers-centric examples use writing book). adamant belief Pittsburgh’s TJ Watt win 2021 Defensive Player Year award, despite many others calling Los Angeles’ Aaron Donald claim title. effort prove point, sought design coined Adjusted Defensive Impact. begin, wanted explore idea defensive sacks created equal, player’s true impact always perfectly represented top-level statistics.account , opted adjust add statistical weight sack statistics. done multiple areas. instance, players competed 17 regular-season games 2021. adjust , took total game played data (2,936) divided 17 (full season) achieve weighted adjustment 0.0058. TJ Watt played just 15 games 2021. adjusted equation, therefore, (17-‘games’) * 0.0058. result? gets bit credit adjustment , say, Myles Garrett played 17 regular-season games.Going model, created weighted adjustment solo sacks (0.90), negative weighted adjustment (-0.14) sack charted “unblocked,” weighted adjustment account many times player actually rushed QB compared many defensive snaps played. Using data SIS Data Hub, full code :end result? Taking account adjusted defensive impact, TJ Watt absolutely dominant 2021 season:
Figure 0.4: Adjusted Defensive Impact
examples - Ben Baldwin’s 4th-model, Football Outsiders’ DVOA, attempt quantify defensive player impact - just leading edge burgeoning analytics movement NFL. Moreover, beauty analytics mathematician statistics buff order enter fray. takes genuine curiosity explore Bob Carroll, Pete Palmer, John Thorn coined “Hidden Game Football” desire learn, already, R programming language.
code:
options(digits = 2)

## selecting just information I want and then renaming
pass.data <- pass_rush_data %>%
  select(Player, Team, Games, `Pass Snaps`, `Pass Rushes`,
         `Solo Sacks`, `Ast. Sacks`, `Comb. Sacks`, 
         `Unblocked Sacks`, Hurries, Hits) %>%
  rename(total.snaps = `Pass Snaps`,
         total.rushes = `Pass Rushes`,
         solo.sacks = `Solo Sacks`,
         asst.sacks = `Ast. Sacks`,
         comb.sacks = `Comb. Sacks`,
         unblocked.sacks = `Unblocked Sacks`,
         player = Player,
         team = Team,
         games = Games,
         hurries = Hurries,
         hits = Hits)

## creating a new column to get percentages of snaps where player rushed
pass.data$rush.percent <- pass.data$total.rushes / pass.data$total.snaps

## getting weights to add to the equation
## for example, a solo sack is "more impressive" than an assisted sack. 
## to account for this, we will take the sum of combined sacks (995) and divide 
## by the sum of solo sacks (892) which is (0.9). Therefore, a weight of 0.9 will
## be placed on solo sacks.

## as well, a negative weight will be applied to unblocked sacks. Using the total
## of solo sacks (892) and dividing by the sum of unblocked sacks (128) gives us a
## NEGATIVE weight of 0.14 to be applied to unblocked sacks

## further, a weight must be applied to those players who played less than a complete season.
## the weight here works out to be 0.0058.

## summarizing information
calculated.impact <- pass.data %>%
  group_by(player) %>%
  summarize(
    adjusted.games = (17 - games) * 0.0058,
    adjusted.solo = solo.sacks * 0.9,
    adjusted.unblocked = unblocked.sacks / -0.14,
    adjusted.rush.percent = 0.81 - rush.percent,
    combined.impact = sum(adjusted.games + (solo.sacks * 0.9) + (unblocked.sacks * -0.14) + adjusted.rush.percent))

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1 nflreadr: An Introduction to the Data
text: important part nflverse , course, data. begin, examine core data underpins nflverse: weekly player stats detailed play-–play data. Using nflreadr, end user able collect weekly top-level stats via load_player_stats() function much robust play--play numbers using load_pbp() function.may imagine, important distinction load_player_stats() load_pbp(). mentioned, load_player_stats() provide weekly, pre-calculated statistics either offense kicking. Conversely, load_pbp() provide 350 metrics every single play every single game dating back 1999.load_player_stats() function includes following offensive information:well, switching stat_type “kicking” provides following information:data returned rich play--play data covering next, load_player_stats() function extremely helpful need quickly (correctly!) recreate official stats listed either NFL’s website Pro Football Reference.example, let’s say need get Ben Roethlisberger’s total passing yard attempts 2021 season. via load_pbp() , need context, using load_player_stats() much efficient.
code:
offensive.stats <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)
ls(offensive.stats)##  [1] "air_yards_share"             "attempts"                   
##  [3] "carries"                     "completions"                
##  [5] "dakota"                      "fantasy_points"             
##  [7] "fantasy_points_ppr"          "interceptions"              
##  [9] "pacr"                        "passing_2pt_conversions"    
## [11] "passing_air_yards"           "passing_epa"                
## [13] "passing_first_downs"         "passing_tds"                
## [15] "passing_yards"               "passing_yards_after_catch"  
## [17] "player_id"                   "player_name"                
## [19] "racr"                        "receiving_2pt_conversions"  
## [21] "receiving_air_yards"         "receiving_epa"              
## [23] "receiving_first_downs"       "receiving_fumbles"          
## [25] "receiving_fumbles_lost"      "receiving_tds"              
## [27] "receiving_yards"             "receiving_yards_after_catch"
## [29] "recent_team"                 "receptions"                 
## [31] "rushing_2pt_conversions"     "rushing_epa"                
## [33] "rushing_first_downs"         "rushing_fumbles"            
## [35] "rushing_fumbles_lost"        "rushing_tds"                
## [37] "rushing_yards"               "sack_fumbles"               
## [39] "sack_fumbles_lost"           "sack_yards"                 
## [41] "sacks"                       "season"                     
## [43] "season_type"                 "special_teams_tds"          
## [45] "target_share"                "targets"                    
## [47] "week"                        "wopr"
kicking.stats <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021, stat_type = "kicking")
ls(kicking.stats)##  [1] "fg_att"              "fg_blocked"          "fg_blocked_distance"
##  [4] "fg_blocked_list"     "fg_long"             "fg_made"            
##  [7] "fg_made_0_19"        "fg_made_20_29"       "fg_made_30_39"      
## [10] "fg_made_40_49"       "fg_made_50_59"       "fg_made_60_"        
## [13] "fg_made_distance"    "fg_made_list"        "fg_missed"          
## [16] "fg_missed_0_19"      "fg_missed_20_29"     "fg_missed_30_39"    
## [19] "fg_missed_40_49"     "fg_missed_50_59"     "fg_missed_60_"      
## [22] "fg_missed_distance"  "fg_missed_list"      "fg_pct"             
## [25] "gwfg_att"            "gwfg_blocked"        "gwfg_distance"      
## [28] "gwfg_made"           "gwfg_missed"         "pat_att"            
## [31] "pat_blocked"         "pat_made"            "pat_missed"         
## [34] "pat_pct"             "player_id"           "player_name"

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1.1 Getting Weekly Player Stats via load_player_stats()
text: familiar R, might seem logical following get Roethlisberger’s total passing yards number attempts 2021 regular season:can see ben.weekly output, matched official 2021 regular stats perfectly 3,740 passing yards 605 attempts. code just created several things. First, using nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021) place data R environment DF titled weekly.data.Next, group data together alike player_id (every individual player unique ID number) well player’s actual name. filtering level, looking just regular season (REG) within season_type also removing quarterbacks except Ben Roethlisberger. important note player names just first initial last name, without space period.filtering regular season, able summarize weekly data combined statistics summing weekly totals passing yards attempts.However, filtering player_name can lead significant issues results. excellent example Josh Allen. Let’s recreate code successfully provided Roethlisberger’s stats, replace Ben Josh Allen:output tells us Allen threw 4,049 yards 603 attempts 2021 regular season. check Pro Football Reference page tells us numbers incorrect. fact, 4,407 passing yards 646 attempts. end 358 passing yards 43 attempts short?answer comes Aaron Schatz, creator Football Outsiders, explained Tweet official Buffalo Bills’ scorer, week 3 NFL season, decided refer Allen “Jos.Allen” result Washington Commanders player named “Jonathan Allen.”double check , can run code , remove player_name filter switch searching just players Buffalo Bills using recent_team.Grouping player_id player_name (well filtering Buffalo), can see , indeed, Josh Allen data twice player_id. Moreover, math, can see numbers two entries add official statistics Pro Football Reference page.
code:
weekly.data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

ben.weekly <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & player_name == "B.Roethlisberger") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'player_id'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
tibble(ben.weekly)## # A tibble: 1 x 4
##   player_id  player_name      total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>      <chr>                  <dbl>      <int>
## 1 00-0022924 B.Roethlisberger        3740        605
josh.allen <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_name) %>%
  filter(player_name == "J.Allen" & season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))

tibble(josh.allen)## # A tibble: 1 x 3
##   player_name total.yards n.attempts
##   <chr>             <dbl>      <int>
## 1 J.Allen            4049        603
two.josh.allens <- weekly.data %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & recent_team == "BUF") %>%
  summarize(total.yards = sum(passing_yards),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts))## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'player_id'. You can override using the
## `.groups` argument.
tibble(two.josh.allens)## # A tibble: 17 x 4
##    player_id  player_name  total.yards n.attempts
##    <chr>      <chr>              <dbl>      <int>
##  1 00-0027685 E.Sanders              0          0
##  2 00-0029000 C.Beasley              0          1
##  3 00-0031588 S.Diggs                0          0
##  4 00-0031787 J.Kumerow              0          0
##  5 00-0033308 M.Breida               0          0
##  6 00-0033466 I.McKenzie             0          0
##  7 00-0033550 D.Webb                 0          0
##  8 00-0033869 M.Trubisky            43          8
##  9 00-0033904 D.Dawkins              0          0
## 10 00-0034857 J.Allen             4049        603
## 11 00-0034857 Jos.Allen            358         43
## 12 00-0035250 D.Singletary           0          0

text: avoid situations, load NFL rosters via nflreadr::load_rosters() function, require unnecessary code order merge two DFs together matching player_id gsis_id number found within roster information. correct issue Josh Allen appearing data different spellings. Instead, write minimal amount code complete task, can following:efficient way gather correct player statistics group_by player_id , despite variation name, player_id remained Josh Allen. order include correct name output, can gather QB names within summarize prior calculating sum passing_yards attempts. , desire see Josh Allen’s number, can filter just name.

josh.allen <- weekly.data %>%

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.1.2.1 An Example: 2021 QB Air Yards per Attempt Leaders
text: example, using group_by combine desired statistics based unique player_id , , avoid issues player names within data. filtering include just statistics regular season, first use summarize function grab first player_name associated player_id. , find two items: (1.) total number passing attempts QB outputted new row titled n.attempts regular season total QB’s air yards, outputted new row titled n.airyards.important note final row created summarize function statistic included within load_player_stats(). order find QB’s average air yards per attempt, must use first two items ’ve created simple division (created n.airyards divided n.attempts).Finally, “clear noise” QBs minimal attempts season, included filter include passers least 400 attempts. , arrange new DF sorting QBs descending order average air yards per attempt.end results look like :Russell Wilson led NFL 2021 9.89 air yards per attempt.
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

ay.per.attempt <- data %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(player_name),
            n.attempts = sum(attempts),
            n.airyards = sum(passing_air_yards),
            ay.attempt = n.airyards / n.attempts) %>%
  filter(n.attempts >= 400) %>%
  select(player_name, ay.attempt) %>%
  arrange(-ay.attempt)
tibble(ay.per.attempt)## # A tibble: 25 x 2
##    player_name   ay.attempt
##    <chr>              <dbl>
##  1 R.Wilson            9.89
##  2 J.Hurts             8.99
##  3 B.Mayfield          8.73
##  4 M.Stafford          8.48
##  5 J.Allen             8.20
##  6 K.Cousins           8.16
##  7 J.Burrow            8.12
##  8 D.Carr              8.12
##  9 T.Brady             8.10
## 10 T.Bridgewater       8.04
## # ... with 15 more rows

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.2.1 An Example: QB Aggressiveness on 3rd Down
text: Sticking air yards example , let’s examine metric created using load_pbp() coined QB 3rd Aggressiveness. metric designed determine QBs NFL aggressive 3rd situations gauging often throw ball , pass, first line. interesting metric explore , just like many metrics NFL, air yards created equal. example, eight air yards 1st 10 less valuable eight air yards 3rd 5.First, let’s highlight code used create results metric break line--line.can see tibble() output results, Dak Prescott aggressive quarterback 3rd passing situations 2021 season, passing , beyond, line gain just 63% time.creating new dataframe called aggressiveness 2021 play--play originally collected using data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021), use group_by ensure data collected per individual quarterback via passer_id.using group_by function lump data individual QB, use filter() function. course, want play_types “pass” 3rd downs. However, code, filtering just 3rd situations yards go five ten yards.personal decision end creating metric, certainly arguments made regarding “capture” scenarios data require “aggressiveness.” logic? less five yards go 3rd , opposing defense able “sell ” pass question offense attempt gain first ground. Conversely, anything ten yards likely results defense selling pass, thus leaving imprint aggressiveness output quarterbacks.sake curiosity, can edit code include passing attempts 3rd 10 yards go first :results quite different first running metric, Dak Prescott now 4th aggressive QB, Kyler Murray moves top approaching nearly 70% aggressiveness rate 3rd . small change highlights important element analytics: much work result coder (ie., ) able justify decision-making process developing filters metric create.case, stand argument including just pass attempts 3rd 5 10 yards go accurate assessment aggressiveness , example, 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation cases.begs question, though: cases 3rd 8 yards go obvious passing situation? example falls guise “garbage time.”
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2021)

aggressiveness <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(-percentage)

tibble(aggressiveness)## # A tibble: 30 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name  team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>        <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 00-0033077 D.Prescott   DAL      84         53      0.631
##  2 00-0035228 K.Murray     ARI      60         37      0.617
##  3 00-0036389 J.Hurts      PHI      65         40      0.615
##  4 00-0033873 P.Mahomes    KC       93         56      0.602
##  5 00-0034855 B.Mayfield   CLE      59         35      0.593
##  6 00-0036971 T.Lawrence   JAX      78         46      0.590
##  7 00-0036355 J.Herbert    LAC      87         51      0.586
##  8 00-0035710 D.Jones      NYG      60         35      0.583
##  9 00-0036212 T.Tagovailoa MIA      60         35      0.583
## 10 00-0026498 M.Stafford   LA       95         54      0.568
## # ... with 20 more rows
aggressiveness.under.10 <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo <= 10) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))

tibble(aggressiveness.under.10)## # A tibble: 33 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name  team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>        <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>

##  5 00-0034857 J.Allen      BUF     138         88      0.638

chapter: 1 An Introduction to NFL Analytics and the R Programming Language (*rough draft)
heading: 1.2.1.1 QB Aggressiveness: Filtering for “Garbage Time?”
text: initial running QB Aggressiveness metric, Josh Allen 15th aggressive QB NFL 3rd 5 10 yards go. much success Buffalo Bills play 15th place ranking?Bills, conclusion 2021 season, largest positive point differential league 194 (Bills scored 483 points, allowing just 289). Perhaps Allen’s numbers skewed Bills often playing lead late game?account , can add information filter() function attempt remove referenced analytics community “garbage time stats.”Let’s add “garbage time” filter code ’ve already prepared:now using code, included three new items filter(). First, stipulating , aside distance inclusion, want plays occurred offense’s win probability 5% 95%, well ensuring plays happen two-minute warning either half.decision range win probability numbers , , personal preference. nflfastR first released, analyst often used 20-80% range win probability. However, Sebastian Carl - one creators nflverse explained package’s Discord:Sebastian Carl: “generally conservative filtering plays using wp. Especially vegas wp model can reach >85% probs early game incorporates market lines. never understood 20% <= wp <= 80%”garbage time” filter. removing ton plays. general advice lower boundary something around 5% (.e., 5% <= wp <= 95%).Ben Baldwin followed Carl’s thoughts:Ben Baldwin: “agree . 20-80% used filter looking run-heavy team (outside range teams change behavior lot). possibly teams behave 4th downs. team player performance.”Based advice, typically stick 5-95% range filtering win probability using play--play data. , case, impact.mentioned, prior filtering garbage time, Allen 15th aggressive QB league nearly 52%. However, filtering garbage time, Allen rose 9th aggressive QB, slight increase percentage 54%.interesting example, though, Dak Prescott Cowboys. Dallas maintained second largest point differential league (530 points 358 points , 172 point difference). Without garbage time filter, Prescott tops NFL aggressiveness rating 63%.adjusted garbage time? Prescott remained atop NFL aggressiveness rating 65.5%.Allen’s increase standings, Prescott remaining best league, specific metric, possible indicator inclusion “garbage time” filters provides slightly accurate result.
code:
aggressiveness.garbage <- data %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(down == 3, play_type == "pass", ydstogo >= 5, ydstogo <= 10,
         wp > .05, wp < .95, half_seconds_remaining > 120) %>%
  summarize(player_name = first(passer),
            team = first(posteam),
            total = n(),
            aggressive = sum(air_yards >= ydstogo, na.rm = TRUE),
            percentage = aggressive / total) %>%
  filter(total >= 50) %>%
  arrange(desc(percentage))

tibble(aggressiveness.garbage)## # A tibble: 26 x 6
##    passer_id  player_name team  total aggressive percentage
##    <chr>      <chr>       <chr> <int>      <int>      <dbl>
##  1 00-0033077 D.Prescott  DAL      61         40      0.656
##  2 00-0036971 T.Lawrence  JAX      51         33      0.647
##  3 00-0035228 K.Murray    ARI      51         31      0.608
##  4 00-0026498 M.Stafford  LA       79         48      0.608
##  5 00-0033873 P.Mahomes   KC       68         41      0.603
##  6 00-0035710 D.Jones     NYG      50         30      0.6  
##  7 00-0036355 J.Herbert   LAC      67         38      0.567
##  8 00-0036972 M.Jones     NE       57         31      0.544
##  9 00-0034857 J.Allen     BUF      59         32      0.542
## 10 00-0029263 R.Wilson    SEA      56         30      0.536
## # ... with 16 more rows

heading: 1.3 Retrieving & Working With Data for Multiple Seasons
text: case load_pbp() load_player_stats(), possible load data multiple seasons.example calculating average air yard per attempt, important note Russell Wilson’s league-leading average 9.89 air yards per attempt calculated using passing attempts, meaning pass attempts complete incomplete.first example working data across multiple seasons, let’s examine average air yards completed passes. begin, retrieve play--play data last five seasons:retrieve multiple seasons data, colon : placed years want. run code, nflreadr output data include play--play data starting oldest season (case, 2017 NFL season):data collected, can run code looks quite similar code explored 2021’s air yards per attempt leaders using load_player_stats(). case, however, including additional filter gather passing attempts results complete passes:QBs least 1,000 complete passes since 2017 season, Jameis Winston highest average air yards per complete pass 8.11.
code:
ay.five.years <- nflreadr::load_pbp(2017:2021)
tibble(ay.five.years)## # A tibble: 243,131 x 372
##    play_id game_id     old_game_id home_team away_team season_type  week posteam
##      <dbl> <chr>       <chr>       <chr>     <chr>     <chr>       <int> <chr>  
##  1       1 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 <NA>   
##  2      37 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  3      73 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  4      97 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  5     118 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  6     153 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  7     174 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  8     207 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 ARI    
##  9     233 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 DET    
## 10     254 2017_01_AR~ 2017091004  DET       ARI       REG             1 DET    
## # ... with 243,121 more rows, and 364 more variables: posteam_type <chr>,
## #   defteam <chr>, side_of_field <chr>, yardline_100 <dbl>, game_date <chr>,
## #   quarter_seconds_remaining <dbl>, half_seconds_remaining <dbl>,
## #   game_seconds_remaining <dbl>, game_half <chr>, quarter_end <dbl>,
## #   drive <dbl>, sp <dbl>, qtr <dbl>, down <dbl>, goal_to_go <dbl>, time <chr>,
## #   yrdln <chr>, ydstogo <dbl>, ydsnet <dbl>, desc <chr>, play_type <chr>,
## #   yards_gained <dbl>, shotgun <dbl>, no_huddle <dbl>, qb_dropback <dbl>, ...
average.airyards <- ay.five.years %>%
  group_by(passer_id) %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG" & complete_pass == 1) %>%
  summarize(player = first(passer_player_name),
            completions = sum(complete_pass),
            air.yards = sum(air_yards),
            average = air.yards / completions) %>%
  filter(completions >= 1000) %>%
  arrange(-average)

tibble(average.airyards)## # A tibble: 22 x 5
##    passer_id  player      completions air.yards average
##    <chr>      <chr>             <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>

##  2 00-0033537 D.Watson           1186      8461    7.13

##  6 00-0034857 J.Allen            1245      8221    6.60

##  8 00-0026498 M.Stafford         1668     10804    6.48

## 10 00-0032950 C.Wentz            1505      9491    6.31
## # ... with 12 more rows

chapter: Appendix
heading: Air Yards
text: Air yards measure ball travels air, line scrimmage, exact point wide receivers catches, catch, football. take consideration amount yardage gained catch wide receiver (yards catch).example, please see illustration. , line scrimmag 20-yardline. QB completes pass caught midfield (50-yardline). catching football, wide receiver able advance ball opposing 30-yardline getting tackled. First foremost, quarterback credited total 50 passing yards play, wide receiver credited .However, air yards better metric explore QB’s true impact play, credited 30 air yards wide receiver credited 20 yards catch.end, quarterbacks higher air yards per attempt generally assumed throwing ball deeper downfield QBs lower air yards per attempt.multiple ways collect data pertaining air yards. However, straightforward way use load_player_stats:example, can see Tom Brady led NFL 2021 regular season comined total 5,821 air yards works average 342 air yards per game.
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

air.yards <- data %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  summarize(
    attempts = sum(attempts),
    name = first(player_name),
    air.yards = sum(passing_air_yards),
    avg.ay = mean(passing_air_yards)) %>%
  filter(attempts >= 100) %>%
  select(name, air.yards, avg.ay) %>%
  arrange(-air.yards)

tibble(air.yards)## # A tibble: 42 x 3
##    name       air.yards avg.ay
##    <chr>          <dbl>  <dbl>
##  1 T.Brady         5821   342.
##  2 J.Allen         5295   311.
##  3 M.Stafford      5094   300.
##  4 D.Carr          5084   299.
##  5 J.Herbert       5069   298.
##  6 P.Mahomes       4825   284.
##  7 T.Lawrence      4732   278.
##  8 D.Prescott      4612   288.
##  9 K.Cousins       4575   286.
## 10 J.Burrow        4225   264.
## # ... with 32 more rows

chapter: Appendix
heading: Average Depth of Target
text: mentioned , QB’s air yards per attempt can highlight whether attempting push ball deeper field counterparts. official name Average Depth Target (ADOT). can easily generate statistic using load_player_stats function within nflreader:seen results, ignore Drew Lock’s 10.1 ADOT just 111 attempts 2021 regular season, Russell Wilson attempted push ball, average, furtherst downfield among QBs atleast 100 attempts.
code:
data <- nflreadr::load_player_stats(2021)

adot <- data %>%
  filter(season_type == "REG") %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>%
  summarize(
    name = first(player_name),
    attempts = sum(attempts),
    air.yards = sum(passing_air_yards),
    adot = air.yards / attempts) %>%
  filter(attempts >= 100) %>%
  arrange(-adot)

tibble(adot)## # A tibble: 42 x 5
##    player_id  name       attempts air.yards  adot
##    <chr>      <chr>         <int>     <dbl> <dbl>

##  5 00-0036389 J.Hurts         432      3882  8.99
##  6 00-0034855 B.Mayfield      418      3651  8.73
##  7 00-0026498 M.Stafford      601      5094  8.48
##  8 00-0031503 J.Winston       161      1340  8.32
##  9 00-0034857 J.Allen         646      5295  8.20
## 10 00-0029604 K.Cousins       561      4575  8.16
